text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic hysterectomy by coagulating and cutting the left round ligament to increase uterine mobility. The left ovary is tracted to expose the utero-ovarian ligament, which is then controlled by coagulation and cutting. The lateral face of the uterus is visible, and the brown ligament is opened. The left uterosacral ligament insertion is controlled through dissection of the left brown ligament.

The surgeon performs the same procedure on the right side of the uterus, coagulating and cutting the right round ligament to increase uterine mobility. The uterus is moved laterally to the left. The peritoneum between the bladder and uterus is incised, revealing connective tissues that are removed to expose the vesico-uterine ligament. The vesico-uterine ligament is cut at its cranial and medial parts, avoiding the ureter, until the anterior part of the uterine manipulator is visible.

The surgeon controls the left uterine artery, a large vessel, ensuring no accidental bleeding during the dry section. The vein is cut, and dissection proceeds posteriorly to reveal the posterior valve of the uterine manipulator. The right fallopian tube and utero-ovarian ligament are then cut, with coagulation applied over two to three centimeters using bipolar energy to prevent bleeding due to the vascular nature of the ligament.

The surgeon identifies and controls the right uterine pedicle, carefully removing connective tissues between the uterus and adnexa to avoid damaging large veins. Coagulation is applied to the uterine arteries and cervico-vaginal vessels on the right side. The cervico-vaginal vein is cut, followed by the cervical vaginal vessel. A colpotomy is performed using a monopolar needle to cut the vagina on the uterine manipulator, ensuring a dry section under direct vision. The left, anterior, and right parts of the vagina are cut, and the right uterosacral ligament is severed. The needle is repositioned to cut the posterior part of the vagina perpendicularly.

The surgeon performs morcellation of a large uterus using a chardonnay knife, cutting the uterus into two pieces under visual control. The cervix is cut, and the hemi-section is completed intra-abdominally. Vaginal forceps are used to extract the hemi-uterus vaginally without damaging the surrounding tissues. The second part of the uterus is also removed under visual control. The procedure concludes with suturing the edge of the vagina, starting laterally.

The surgeon sutures the left part of the vagina, incorporating peritoneal tissue into the closure using extracorporeal knotting with a 40mm needle and zero vicryl suture. The same technique is applied to the right side, ensuring the entire vaginal mucosa is included. The surgeon uses an X knot for the final closure, completing the vaginal closure with a rubber knot for a secure finish.
